# Transcrição: 07-Jake and the Big Green Slide

- **Fonte:** 07-Jake and the Big Green Slide
- **Duração:** 05:53
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

I'm walking across an open field at a YMCA camp on a September afternoon alongside my fifth grade student Jake, who are heading towards the big green slide. Jake is a ten-year-old boy, he's a kid with autism. If you were in my class for a while, you wouldn't really notice him at first. His primary problems in terms of autism is he doesn't pick up on social cues very well, and he is the most inflexible person I have met in my life. If Jake is in the bathroom and he decides that there's some kind of threat in the hallway, Jake will never come out of the bathroom for the rest of his life unless he determines that the hallway is a safe place. Jake has told me he is not going on the big green slide. Jake doesn't know that he's walking next to me, a person just as inflexible as he is. I don't have autism, I'm just an asshole, and he's gonna go on the big green side today whether he likes it or not. It is one of those moments where an immovable object and an irresistible force are going to collide and I am going to win. Now I don't want Jake to go on the slide because I'm an asshole, I really want him to go because I don't want him to experience regret. I don't want him to miss out on an opportunity. When I was a kid, my parents took a very aggressive disinterest in my entire life. I was a drop-off kid. We will drop you off somewhere, find a ride home, we don't know what you're doing while you're there and we don't care. The result was oftentimes I needed a parent to push me into doing something or cajoling me into doing it and instead there was just nobody there. When I got dropped off for my first little league tryout, I remember walking across the parking lot and I heard the first ping of the ball off the aluminum bat and I thought to myself, I've never made a ping that loud before. Then I saw the kids throwing the ball across the diamond and I thought to myself, I've never thrown a ball that hard across the diamond before. I decided I wasn't trying out for a little league and by the time I got to the registration table, I told the coach, I said, I can't try out today, my knee hurts. The coach looked at me and said, okay. They put me in farm league. I was twice as big as every kid. I pummeled them all and it was an embarrassment all season long. I hated it and I don't want this to happen to Jake. I don't want him to be afraid to do something because someone won't push him. Now I've got some strategies planned and the first one is happening right now. We're walking towards the big green slide. Jake doesn't pick up on social cues. Any other kid would know I'm leading him down the path of destruction. They would know that this is a trap but Jake has autism so he doesn't see the trap I'm laying out for him as we walk to that big green slide. Now it's not really fair to call it a slide. It's really a 70 foot long green tube that runs the length of a hill at like a 35 degree angle. It's a serious piece of business. I've gone down the big green slide. It scares the shit out of me and so Jake is going to be scared too but I want him to do this. We get up to the line and we're waiting in line at the big green slide because there's always a line at the big green slide. And as we get closer and closer to looking at the slide, which is what Jake thinks we're about to do, I use my next trick.

## [03:01 - 05:53]

It's a trick that a mother taught me years ago as we're one away from the slide. I lean close to Jake and I say, listen Jake, if we don't go down the slide today, the man is going to be mad at us. There is no man. There's never a man. But it turns out if you tell a kid that the man is going to be mad at you, they just do it. They believe that in the world there's just a man waiting to punish you. There's no man at the slide. There's a 16 year old girl who's just nodding when you're allowed to go down the slide. But it works. I can feel Jake starting to move forward and before he has a chance to say anything, I put my hand on his shoulder and I lower him down to the side and I say, listen Jake, don't worry, I'm going to go down with you. I'm going to wrap my legs around you and my arms around you. We're going to go down together. Everything's going to be fine. Now I do that. I get behind him and I put my legs and my arms around him. I can feel his heart beating like a hummingbird and his muscles are tense. And I say, okay Jake, are you ready? And he doesn't say anything, which I take as a yes. And then I push Jake down the big green slide. I think it's a wonderful decision. And then I hear Jake begin to scream in terror, a single word. Why? And I jump into the slide to follow him, but the girl who's apparently the man, because now I'm listening to her, says, you can't go yet. You have to wait till he gets to the bottom. And I'm worried because Jake has autism. And when he hits the bottom, if he's been triggered, which he probably is because I have completely betrayed him and lied to him and shoved him down the most terrifying thing he's ever had in his life, he's going to like run for the woods or the water and I'm going to lose him. So I'm waiting for the man, which is a girl, to tell me to go. And when she says go, I go down the slide as fast as I can. And when I reach the bottom, my fears are realized because I don't see Jake. And I look to the tree line and I look to the water and I cannot see Jake wherever I look. And I start to panic and my heart is beating as fast as his. And when I stand up, I finally see Jake. I turn around and he's running back up the slide, up the stairs with a boy in his hand, the two of them running together. Now don't clap because the ends do not justify the means. That could have gone really poorly. And you should not shove kids down slides when you promise them that you're going to go with them. But I didn't want Jake to miss this chance. And I didn't want him to be the kid who had to go down with the teacher every time. I wanted Jake to do something that I thought he could do. And as a teacher sometimes we push kids to do things that they don't think they can do. And sometimes we push them to do things that we are not quite sure that they can do. Because I think it's better to push those kids as far as you can see rather than letting them live with regret and missed opportunities. Thank you.
